{{Short description|Dimension of vulnerability to multiple stressors}}
{{Multiple issues|
{{Citation style|date=November 2014}}
{{Lead too short|date=December 2022}}
}}
In its broadest sense, '''social vulnerability''' is one dimension of [[vulnerability]] to multiple [[stressor]]s and [[Acute stress reaction|shock]]s, including [[abuse]], [[social exclusion]] and [[natural hazards]]. Social vulnerability refers to the [[disadvantaged|inability of people]], organizations, and societies to withstand adverse impacts from multiple stressors to which they are exposed. These impacts are due in part to characteristics inherent in [[social interaction]]s, [[institution]]s, and systems of [[value (ethics)|cultural values]].

Social vulnerability is an interdisciplinary topic that connects social, health, and environmental fields of study. As it captures the susceptibility of a system or an individual to external stressors such as [[pandemic]]s or [[natural disaster]]s, social vulnerability is a focus of many studies in the [[risk management]] literature.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.4324/9780203351628-11 |chapter=Social Systems, Ecological Networks and Disasters: Toward a Socio-Political Ecology of Disasters |title=Hurricane Andrew |date=2012 |pages=40–55 |isbn=978-0-203-35162-8 |first1=Nicole |last1=Dashy |first2=Walter Gillis |last2=Peacock |first3=Betty Hearn |last3=Morrow |editor-last1=Gillis Peacock |editor-last2=Gladwin |editor-last3=Hearn Morrow |editor-first1=Walter |editor-first2=Hugh |editor-first3=Betty }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Anderson |first1=Mary B |title=Rising From the Ashes: Development Strategies in Times of Disaster |last2=Woodrow |first2=Peter J |publisher=IT Publications |year=1998 |isbn=978-1-85339-439-3 |location=London |oclc=878098209}}{{page needed|date=April 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite report |url=http://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/636921468765021121/pdf/multi0page.pdf |title=Vulnerability: a view from different disciplines |last1=Alwang |first1=Jeffrey |last2=Siegel |first2=PaulB. |last3=Jorgensen |first3=Steen |date=June 2001}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Conway |first1=Tim |last2=Norton |first2=Andy |date=November 2002 |title=Nets, Ropes, Ladders and Trampolines: The Place of Social Protection within Current Debates on Poverty Reduction |journal=Development Policy Review |volume=20 |issue=5 |pages=533–540 |doi=10.1111/1467-7679.00188 }}</ref>

== Background ==
The structural nature, as opposed to the individual level, is central to social vulnerability.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Ang |last2=Toll |first2=Mathew |last3=Bentley |first3=Rebecca |title=Mapping social vulnerability indicators to understand the health impacts of climate change: a scoping review |journal=The Lancet Planetary Health |date=2023 |volume=7 |issue=11 |pages=e925–e937 |doi=10.1016/S2542-5196(23)00216-4 |pmid=37940212 |url=https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanplh/article/PIIS2542-5196(23)00216-4/fulltext|doi-access=free }}</ref> Social and political systemic inequalities influence or shape the susceptibility of various groups to harm as well as govern their ability to respond.<ref name=":12">{{cite journal |last1=Cutter |first1=Susan L. |last2=Boruff |first2=Bryan J. |last3=Shirley |first3=W. Lynn |title=Social Vulnerability to Environmental Hazards * |journal=Social Science Quarterly |date=June 2003 |volume=84 |issue=2 |pages=242–261 |doi=10.1111/1540-6237.8402002 }}</ref> Both the sensitivity and resilience of a group to prepare, cope, and recover from hazards define their social vulnerability.<ref name=":3">{{cite journal |last1=Turner |first1=B. L. |last2=Kasperson |first2=Roger E. |last3=Matson |first3=Pamela A. |last4=McCarthy |first4=James J. |last5=Corell |first5=Robert W. |last6=Christensen |first6=Lindsey |last7=Eckley |first7=Noelle |last8=Kasperson |first8=Jeanne X. |last9=Luers |first9=Amy |last10=Martello |first10=Marybeth L. |last11=Polsky |first11=Colin |last12=Pulsipher |first12=Alexander |last13=Schiller |first13=Andrew |title=A framework for vulnerability analysis in sustainability science |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=8 July 2003 |volume=100 |issue=14 |pages=8074–8079 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1231335100 |doi-access=free |pmid=12792023 |pmc=166184 }}</ref>

Although considerable research attention has examined components of biophysical vulnerability and the vulnerability of the built environment,<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Mileti |first=Dennis D. |title=Disasters by Design: A Reassessment of Natural Hazards in the United States |date=18 June 1999 |publisher=Washington DC: Joseph Henry Press. |isbn=978-0-309-26173-9}}</ref> we once knew the least about the social aspects of vulnerability.<ref name=":12"/> Socially created vulnerabilities were largely ignored, mainly due to the difficulty in quantifying them.

Researching social vulnerability is interdisciplinary, combining theories from sociology, health, political economy, and geography.<ref name=":02">{{cite journal |last1=Cutter |first1=Susan L. |title=The origin and diffusion of the social vulnerability index (SoVI) |journal=International Journal of Disaster Risk Reduction |date=July 2024 |volume=109 |article-number=104576 |doi=10.1016/j.ijdrr.2024.104576 |bibcode=2024IJDRR.10904576C |doi-access=free }}</ref> Just like the different disciplines use different approaches and scopes of analyses (qualitative or quantitative; different objects/groups of analysis; different types of hazards/stressors), so too did the early versions of attempting to quantify social vulnerability.

Since the 1960s, there have been methods for collecting and quantifying data to depict a community's social conditions and quality of life.<ref name=":02"/> Within the geography discipline, spatially quantifying social problems and social well-being has been practiced since the 1970s.<ref name=":02"/> At the same time, [[Phil O'Keefe]], Ken Westgate, and Ben Wisner introduced the concept of vulnerability within the discourse on natural hazards and disasters, emphasizing the role of socio-economic conditions as causes of disasters.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=O'Keefe |first1=Phil |last2=Westgate |first2=Ken |last3=Wisner |first3=Ben |title=Taking the naturalness out of natural disasters |journal=Nature |date=15 April 1976 |volume=260 |issue=5552 |pages=566–567 |doi=10.1038/260566a0 |bibcode=1976Natur.260..566O |doi-access=free }}</ref>  [[Susan Cutter]]'s 2003 social vulnerability index was a turning point in studying social vulnerability. The index and hazard of place model was built upon the decades-before groundwork and synthesized the interdisciplinary challenges and goals of measuring vulnerability. As of March 2024, Cutter's original paper has been cited over 7,500 times, suggesting its influence across fields and potential replication of its methodology in different contexts.<ref name=":02"/>

It is important to consider, however, that analyses focusing on stress-to-vulnerability are insufficient for understanding the impacts on and responses of affected groups.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite book |last1=White |first1=Gilbert F. |title=Assessment of Research on Natural Hazards |last2=Haas |first2=J. Eugene |date=May 15, 1975 |publisher=The MIT Press |isbn=978-0-262-08083-5}}{{page needed|date=April 2025}}</ref> These issues are often underlined in attempts to model the concept (see Models of Social Vulnerability).

==Definitions and Types==
"Vulnerability" derives from the Latin word ''vulnerare'' (to wound) and describes the potential to be harmed physically and/or psychologically. Vulnerability is often understood as the counterpart of [[Psychological resilience|resilience]], and is increasingly studied in linked [[social-ecological systems]]. [[The Yogyakarta Principles]], one of the [[international human rights instruments]], uses the term "vulnerability" to refer to such potential for abuse or [[social exclusion]].<ref>The Yogyakarta Principles, Principles 9, 11, and 15</ref>

The concept of social vulnerability emerged most recently within the discourse on natural hazards and disasters. To date, no one definition has been agreed upon. Similarly, multiple theories of social vulnerability exist.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Weichselgartner|first=Juergen|date=1 May 2001|title=Disaster mitigation: the concept of vulnerability revisited|journal=Disaster Prevention and Management|volume=10|issue=2|pages=85–95|doi=10.1108/09653560110388609|bibcode=2001DisPM..10...85W }}</ref> Most work conducted so far focuses on empirical observation and conceptual models. Thus, current social vulnerability research is a [[Middle range theory (sociology)| middle-range theory]] and represents an attempt to understand the social conditions that transform a natural hazard (e.g., floods, earthquakes, mass movements) into a social disaster. The concept emphasizes two central themes:
#Both the causes and the phenomenon of disasters are defined by social processes and structures. Thus, it is not only a geo- or biophysical hazard, but rather the social context that needs to be considered to understand "natural" disasters.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cutter |first1=Susan |last2=Hewitt |first2=K. |title=Interpretations of Calamity from the Viewpoint of Human Ecology |journal=Geographical Review |date=April 1984 |volume=74 |issue=2 |page=226 |doi=10.2307/214106 |jstor=214106 |bibcode=1984GeoRv..74..226C }}</ref>
#Although different groups of a society may share a similar exposure to a natural hazard, the hazard has varying consequences for these groups, since they have diverging capacities and abilities to handle the impact of a hazard.

=== Types ===

==== Vulnerability to natural hazards, or climate vulnerability ====
{{Main|Climate change vulnerability}}
[[Natural hazards]] reveal the level of social vulnerability of individuals and communities. The way people or communities can "respond to, cope with, recover from, and adapt to hazards" can indicate the degree of vulnerability.<ref name=":12"/> In the wake of a disaster event, factors like economic, demographic, and housing conditions can determine [[Climate vulnerability|vulnerability]], [[adaptive capacity]], and [[preparedness]]. Flooding, for example, will affect a homeowner whose basement has flooded differently from a renter whose basement apartment has also flooded.

==== Collective vulnerability, or community vulnerability ====
Collective vulnerability is a state in which the integrity and social fabric of a community is or was threatened through traumatic events or repeated collective violence.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Abramowitz |first=Sharon A. |date=2005 |title=The poor have become rich, and the rich have become poor: Collective trauma in the Guinean Languette |journal=Social Science & Medicine |volume=61 |issue=10 |pages=2106–2118 |doi=10.1016/j.socscimed.2005.03.023 |pmid=16125293}}</ref> In addition, according to the collective vulnerability hypothesis, shared experience of vulnerability and the loss of shared normative references can lead to collective reactions aimed to reestablish the lost norms and trigger forms of collective [[Psychological resilience|resilience]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Elcheroth |first=Guy |date=2006 |title=Individual-level and community-level effects of war trauma on social representations related to humanitarian law |journal=European Journal of Social Psychology |language=en |volume=36 |issue=6 |pages=907–930 |doi=10.1002/ejsp.330 }}</ref>

Social psychologists have developed this theory to study the support for [[human rights]]. It is rooted in the consideration that devastating collective events are sometimes followed by claims for measures that may prevent a similar event from happening again. For instance, the [[Universal Declaration of Human Rights]] was a direct consequence of the horrors of [[World War II]]. Psychological research by [[Willem Doise]] and colleagues shows indeed that after people have experienced a collective injustice, they are more likely to support the reinforcement of human rights.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Doise |first1=Willem |last2=Spini |first2=Dario |last3=Clémence |first3=Alain |title=Human rights studied as social representations in a cross-national context |journal=European Journal of Social Psychology |date=February 1999 |volume=29 |issue=1 |pages=1–29 |doi=10.1002/(SICI)1099-0992(199902)29:1<1::AID-EJSP909>3.0.CO;2-# }}</ref> Populations who collectively endured systematic human rights violations are more critical of national authorities and less tolerant of rights violations.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Elcheroth |first1=Guy |last2=Spini |first2=Dario |date=2009 |title=Public Support for the Prosecution of Human Rights Violations in the Former Yugoslavia |journal=Peace and Conflict: Journal of Peace Psychology |volume=15 |issue=2 |pages=189–214 |doi=10.1080/10781910902837321 |issn=1078-1919}}</ref> Some analyses performed by Dario Spini, Guy Elcheroth and Rachel Fasel<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Spini |first1=Dario |last2=Elcheroth |first2=Guy |last3=Fasel |first3=Rachel |date=2008 |title=The Impact of Group Norms and Generalization of Risks across Groups on Judgments of War Behavior |journal=Political Psychology |language=en |volume=29 |issue=6 |pages=919–941 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9221.2008.00673.x |issn=1467-9221 |doi-access=free}}</ref> on the Red Cross' "People on War" survey shows that when individuals have direct experience with the armed conflict are less keen to support humanitarian norms. However, in countries where most social groups in conflict experience similar levels of victimization, people express a greater need to reestablish protective social norms, such as human rights, regardless of the conflict's magnitude.

==Models==
[[File:RH model.svg|350px|thumb|right|Risk-Hazard (RH) model,<ref name=":3" /> showing the impact of a hazard as a function of exposure and sensitivity. The chain sequence begins with the hazard, and the concept of vulnerability is noted implicitly as represented by white arrows.]]

=== Risk-Hazard (RH) Model ===
:Initial RH models sought to understand the impact of a hazard as a function of exposure to the hazardous event and the sensitivity of the entity exposed.<ref name=":3" /> Applications of this model in environmental and climate impact assessments generally emphasised exposure and sensitivity to perturbations and stressors and worked from the hazard to the impacts.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Burton |first1=I |title=The Environment as Hazard |last2=Kates |first2=R.W. |last3=White |first3=G.F. |date=1978 |publisher=Oxford: Oxford University Press |edition=1st}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite book |last=Kates |first=R.W. |title=Hazard Assessment: Art, Science, and Ideology |date=1985 |publisher=Westview Press |pages=251–264}}</ref> However, several inadequacies became apparent. Primarily, it does not address how the systems in question amplify or attenuate the hazard's impacts.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Martine |first1=George |last2=Guzman |first2=Jose Miguel |date=2002 |title=Population, Poverty, and Vulnerability: Mitigating the Effects of Natural Disasters |url=https://www.wilsoncenter.org/sites/default/files/media/documents/publication/Report_8_Martine_%2526_Guzman.pdf |journal=ECSP Report |volume=Summer |issue=8 |pages=45–68}}</ref> Neither does the model address the distinction among exposed subsystems and components that lead to significant variations in the consequences of the hazards, or the role of political economy in shaping differential exposure and consequences.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":6" /> This led to the development of the PAR model.

=== Pressure and Release (PAR) Model ===
[[File:PAR model.pdf|450px|thumb|right|Pressure and Release (PAR) model after Blaikie et al. (1994) showing the progression of vulnerability.<ref name=":7">{{Cite book |last1=Blaikie |first1=P |title=At Risk: Natural Hazards, People's Vulnerability & Disaster |last2=Cannon |first2=T |last3=Davies |first3=I |last4=Wisner |first4=B |date=1994 |publisher=London: Routledge}}</ref> The diagram shows a disaster as the intersection between socio-economic pressures on the left and physical exposures (natural hazards) on the right.]]

:The PAR model understands a disaster as the intersection between socio-economic pressure and physical exposure. Risk is explicitly defined as a function of the perturbation, stressor, or stress and the vulnerability of the exposed unit.<ref name=":7" /> In this way, it directs attention to the conditions that make exposure unsafe, leading to vulnerability and to the causes creating these conditions. Used primarily to address social groups facing disasters, the model emphasises distinctions in vulnerability across different exposure units, such as social class and ethnicity. The model distinguishes between three components on the social side: root causes, dynamic pressures, and unsafe conditions, and one component on the natural side, the natural hazards themselves. Principal root causes include "economic, demographic and political processes" that affect the allocation and distribution of resources among different groups of people. Dynamic Pressures translate economic and political processes in local circumstances (e.g., migration patterns). Unsafe conditions are the specific forms in which vulnerability is expressed in time and space, such as those induced by the physical environment, local economy, or social relations.<ref name=":7" />
:
:Although explicitly highlighting vulnerability, the PAR model appears insufficiently comprehensive for the broader concerns of sustainability science.<ref name=":3" /> Primarily, it does not address the coupled human environment system in the sense of considering the vulnerability of biophysical subsystems, and it provides little detail on the structure of the hazard's causal sequence.<ref name=":3" /> The model also tends to underplay feedback beyond the system of analysis that the integrative RH models included.<ref name=":5">Wisner, B., P. Blaikie, T. Cannon, and I. Davis. 2004. At Risk:  Natural Hazards, People's Vulnerability and Disasters. New York: Routledge.</ref><ref name=":4" />

==== Hazards of Place Model ====
[[Susan Cutter]]'s hazards of place (HOP) model conceptualizes how both physical and social systems shape susceptibility to harm.<ref name=":02"/> Physical characteristics of a landscape can determine the level of exposure to hazards (i.e., elevation, proximity) while social vulnerability depends upon several social determinants of well-being (i.e., socio-economic status, governance).<ref name=":02" /> The HOP model allows for a spatial interaction ('place-based') between the biophysical and the social dimensions of vulnerability that may vary over space and time.<ref name=":02" /> The HOP demonstrates the equal importance of biophysical and social environments in determining the overall vulnerability of a particular area or group.

== Indexes ==
One way to estimate social vulnerability is to use a [[vulnerability index]] that aggregates social factors into a single measurement. Social vulnerability indices are widely used in [[disaster planning]], [[environmental science]], and [[health sciences]].<ref name=":03">{{cite journal |last1=Mah |first1=Jasmine Cassy |last2=Penwarden |first2=Jodie Lynn |last3=Pott |first3=Henrique |last4=Theou |first4=Olga |last5=Andrew |first5=Melissa Kathryn |title=Social vulnerability indices: a scoping review |journal=BMC Public Health |date=28 June 2023 |volume=23 |issue=1 |page=1253 |doi=10.1186/s12889-023-16097-6 |doi-access=free |pmid=37380956 |pmc=10304642 }}</ref> The use of social vulnerability indexes are frequently used in research studies to predict outcomes of illness, like [[COVID-19]] infection, or mortality from disasters or environmental circumstances.<ref name=":03" /> An index allows for a [[Continuous data|continuous]] estimation of social vulnerability that can capture more than a single explanatory variable.<ref name=":03" /> The challenge and discrepancies between different indexes rest with the methodology of how the aggregated variables are chosen. Some researchers use more qualitative methods, such as theory-based approaches or community consultations. In contrast, others use more quantitative statistical methods, such as [[factor analysis]] or [[principal component analysis]], drawing on [[census]]es or similar national surveys.

In 2003, [[Susan Cutter]] created the Social Vulnerability Index (SoVI) using both qualitative and quantitative methods - firstly, by outlining the many potential variables that could contribute to social vulnerability supported by a literature review, and secondly, by condensing the list of over 250 variables into 42 variables that were used in a factor analysis.<ref name=":12"/> After further statistical testing, Cutter and her colleagues found 11 variables that could explain over 75% of the variance of social vulnerability to environmental hazards across U.S. counties.<ref name=":12" />

Since the SoVI was created, many other researchers have used it or developed their own indices, adapting it to local environments and data availability. For example, in Canada, researchers at the [[University of Waterloo]] have created a SoVI for the Canadian context, including ethnicity (language, immigration, and Indigenous categories), visible minorities, and specific built-environment data, using sources unique to Canada.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Chakraborty |first1=Liton |last2=Rus |first2=Horatiu |last3=Henstra |first3=Daniel |last4=Thistlethwaite |first4=Jason |last5=Scott |first5=Daniel |title=A place-based socio-economic status index: Measuring social vulnerability to flood hazards in the context of environmental justice |journal=International Journal of Disaster Risk Reduction |date=February 2020 |volume=43 |article-number=101394 |doi=10.1016/j.ijdrr.2019.101394 |bibcode=2020IJDRR..4301394C }}</ref>

The results of social vulnerability indices can be mapped using [[GIS]] to visualize who may be most vulnerable within study areas.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Holderman |first=Eric |date=November 6, 2014 |title=How GIS Can Aid Emergency Management |url=https://www.govtech.com/public-safety/gis-can-aid-emergency-management.html}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |title=CDC Social Vulnerability Index |url=https://www.fema.gov/node/cdc-social-vulnerability-index}}</ref> Mapping social vulnerability visually identifies at-risk areas which can help inform members of the public, policymakers, and elected officials for better management (preparation, support, and recovery) of hazards.<ref name=":22" />

== Integration into risk planning and adaptation ==
[[File:Social Vulnerability Index variables grouped into four themes.png|thumb|CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index variables grouped into four themes]]
[[File:SVI Timeline-Graphic V7.jpg|thumb|Timeline shows the years CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index changed its database – 2000, 2010, 2014, and 2020.]]
Social vulnerability is increasingly being integrated into disaster preparedness by government agencies and other organizations. This is being done in relation to climate and health vulnerabilities, disaster planning, and adaptation.

During the [[COVID-19 pandemic]], the [[British Red Cross]] created a COVID-19 Vulnerability Index that combines health, demographic, and social vulnerability data with data on digital exclusion and health inequalities. The index was then mapped to represent vulnerable areas across the UK spatially.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020 |title=Identifying vulnerabilities and people at risk in an emergency |url=https://www.redcross.org.uk/-/media/documents-indexed/identifying-vulnerabilities-and-people-at-risk-in-an-emergency.pdf?sc_lang=en |website=British Red Cross}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020 |title=British Red Cross COVID-19 Vulnerability Index Interactive View |url=https://www.arcgis.com/home/item.html?id=4b599f94d2d04d6496cc8b2d89911f62 |website=ArcGIS}}</ref>

In the [[United States]], the [[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention|Centre for Disease Control and Prevention]] (CDC) and the [[Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry]] (ATSDR) have developed a place-based social vulnerability index (SVI) alongside an interactive mapping application.<ref name=":04">{{Cite web |title=Social Vulnerability Index |url=https://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/place-health/php/svi/index.html |access-date=April 2, 2025 |website=Place and Health - Geospatial Research, Analysis, and Services Program (GRASP)|date=22 October 2024 }}</ref> Public health officials use the index to identify where there is need for emergency shelters and to determine how many supplies are needed to distribute.<ref name=":04" /> State and local health departments, in addition to non-profits, use the index to promote health initiatives.<ref name=":04" /> In 2023, [[Federal Emergency Management Agency|FEMA]] integrated the CDC/ATSDR's social vulnerability index into their National Risk Index - a mapping tool representing the risk associated with 18 natural hazards.<ref>{{Cite web |date=March 30, 2023 |title=FEMA Updated National Risk Index to Incorporate Social Vulnerability Data |url=https://eelp.law.harvard.edu/tracker/fema-updated-national-risk-index-to-incorporate-social-vulnerability-data/ |website=Environmental & Energy Law Program: Harvard Law School}}</ref> This integration informs emergency planners to best distribute numbers of emergency personnel to at-risk areas, as well as plan evacuation routes.<ref name=":04" />

In southern [[California]], where wildfires have been increasing in frequency and destruction, the [[American Red Cross]] has used social vulnerability mapping in their campaign "Prepare SoCal" to highlight communities at-risk and point to where may be strategic to invest in preparedness education, tools, and resources for greater resilience.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025 |title=Mapping Vulnerability: Where is the need? |url=https://www.redcross.org/local/california/preparesocal/about-prepare/mapping-vulnerability.html?srsltid=AfmBOoogBDO743dG2WKP2x0WAdgq_Kj0Htty0e2t2mMCwM8ZOOW9g6Dp |website=American Red Cross}}</ref>

The [[European Environment Agency]] has developed its own social vulnerability index tool that combines social, economic, and environmental indicators and associated data to highlight vulnerability to climate change.<ref name=":13">{{Cite web |date=May 17, 2024 |title=Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) |url=https://climate-adapt.eea.europa.eu/en/mission/solutions/tools/social-vulnerability-index-svi |website=EU Mission on Adaptation to Climate Change Portal}}</ref> It can be used in conjunction with geographic layers that include flood risk and thermal heat data, to draw connections between social vulnerability and climate vulnerability explicitly.<ref name=":13" /> This tool has been used in cities and counties across Europe, including cities in Ireland and Spain, in addition to projects in Athens and Milan.<ref name=":13" /> The use of the index allows cities to plan future adaptation measures, understand how climate impacts may affect their neighbourhoods differently, and raise awareness among their citizens.<ref name=":13" />

In [[Australia]], the [[University of Melbourne]]'s School of Population and Global Health has created a country-wide social vulnerability index to assess how social factors affect human health vulnerability to climate change.<ref name=":23">{{Cite web |date=2023 |title=Inequalities and climate change: developing an index of human health vulnerability to climate change in Australia |url=https://mspgh.unimelb.edu.au/research-groups/centre-for-health-policy/healthy-housing/inequalities-and-climate-change |website=The University of Melbourne School of Population and Global Health}}</ref> Their index uses over 70 indicators, many relating directly to climate change and extreme weather.<ref name=":23" /> The index is publicly available and was designed for communities, emergency response planners, and public health officials to better prepare for and recover from climate and weather disasters across Australia.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Li |first1=Ang |last2=Toll |first2=Mathew |last3=Bentley |first3=Rebecca |date=February 22, 2024 |title=We aren't all equal when it comes to climate vulnerability |url=https://pursuit.unimelb.edu.au/articles/we-aren-t-all-equal-when-it-comes-to-climate-vulnerability |website=Pursuit (The University of Melbourne)}}</ref>

==Criticism==
Some authors criticise the conceptualisation of social vulnerability for overemphasising the social, political, and economic processes and structures that lead to vulnerable conditions. Inherent in such a view is the tendency to understand people as passive victims<ref name=":6">{{Cite book |last=Hewitt |first=Kenneth |title=Regions of Risk: A Geographical Introduction to Disasters |date=February 7, 1997 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-582-21005-9}}</ref> and to neglect the subjective and intersubjective interpretation and perception of disastrous events. The author, Greg Bankoff, criticises the very basis of the concept, since, in his view, it is shaped by a knowledge system developed within the academic environment of Western countries and therefore inevitably represents the values and principles of that culture. According to Bankoff, the ultimate aim underlying this concept is to depict large parts of the world as dangerous and hostile to provide further justification for interference and intervention.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bankoff |first=Greg |title=Cultures of Disaster: Society and Natural Hazard in the Philippines |publisher=Routledge |year=2003 |isbn=978-0-203-22189-1}}</ref>

There are also criticisms surrounding the use of indices to measure social vulnerability.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Rufat |first=Samuel |last2=Tate |first2=Eric |last3=Emrich |first3=Christopher T. |last4=Antolini |first4=Federico |date=2019-07-04 |title=How Valid Are Social Vulnerability Models? |journal=Annals of the American Association of Geographers |volume=109 |issue=4 |pages=1131–1153 |doi=10.1080/24694452.2018.1535887 |issn=2469-4452 |pmc=11611389 |pmid=39624068}}</ref> Difficulties of standardization, weighting, and aggregation of indicators can affect the quality of an index's results.<ref name=":06">{{cite journal |last1=Barnett |first1=Jon |last2=Lambert |first2=Simon |last3=Fry |first3=Ian |title=The Hazards of Indicators: Insights from the Environmental Vulnerability Index |journal=Annals of the Association of American Geographers |date=5 February 2008 |volume=98 |issue=1 |pages=102–119 |doi=10.1080/00045600701734315 |bibcode=2008AnAAG..98..102B }}</ref> Especially when indexes are used in large scale analyses - to evaluate multiple different countries and/or are using multiple data sources - how representative the results are can be questionable. If an index's results are too broad and subsequently used to guide policy, it can lead to [[maladaptation]].<ref name=":06" /> Some argue that vulnerability is context-dependent, and cannot be categorized and captured fully in indexes, favouring instead smaller-scale empirical investigation.<ref name=":06" />

==See also==
*[[Disadvantaged]]
*[[Minority group]]
*[[Vulnerability index]]
*[[Vulnerability assessment]]

==References==
===Notes===
{{Reflist}}

===Sources===
*Bankoff, G. (2003). Cultures of Disaster: Society and natural hazards in the Philippines. London, RoutledgeCurzon.
*[[Piers Blaikie|Blaikie, P.]], T. Cannon, I. Davis & B. Wisner. (1994). At Risk: Natural hazards, People's vulnerability, and disasters. London, Routledge.
*Cannon, T., J. Twigg, et al. (2005). Social Vulnerability, Sustainable Livelihoods and Disasters, Report to DFID Conflict and Humanitarian Assistance Department (CHAD) and Sustainable Livelihoods Support Office. London, DFID: 63.
*Chambers, R. (1989). "Editorial Introduction: Vulnerability, Coping and Policy." IDS Bulletin 20(2): 7.
* {{cite journal |last1=Sánchez-González |first1=Diego |title=Envejecimiento vulnerable en hogares inundables y su adaptación al cambio climático en ciudades de América Latina: el caso de Monterrey |trans-title=Vulnerable aging in flooded households and adaptation to climate change in cities in Latin America: the case of Monterrey |language=es |journal=Papeles de Población |date=31 December 2016 |volume=22 |issue=90 |pages=9–42 |doi=10.22185/24487147.2016.90.033 }}
*{{cite journal |last1=Cutter |first1=Susan L. |last2=Boruff |first2=Bryan J. |last3=Shirley |first3=W. Lynn |title=Social Vulnerability to Environmental Hazards |journal=Social Science Quarterly |date=2003 |volume=84 |issue=2 |pages=242–261 |doi=10.1111/1540-6237.8402002 }}
*{{cite journal |last1=Cutter |first1=Susan L. |last2=Mitchell |first2=Jerry T. |last3=Scott |first3=Michael S. |title=Revealing the Vulnerability of People and Places: A Case Study of Georgetown County, South Carolina |journal=Annals of the Association of American Geographers |date=1 December 2000 |volume=90 |issue=4 |pages=713–737 |doi=10.1111/0004-5608.00219 }}
*Frankenberger, T. R., M. Drinkwater, et al. (2000). Operationalizing household livelihood security: a holistic approach for addressing poverty and vulnerability. Forum on Operationalising Sustainable Livelihoods Approaches. Pontignano (Siena), FAO.
*Henninger, N. (1998). Mapping and Geographic Analysis of Human Welfare and Poverty: Review and Assessment. Washington DC, World Resources Institute.
*Hewitt, K., Ed. (1983). Interpretation of Calamity: From the Viewpoint of Human Ecology. Boston, Allen.
*Hewitt, K. (1997). Regions of Risk: A Geographical Introduction to Disasters. Essex, Longman.
*{{cite journal |last1=O'Keefe |first1=Phil |last2=Westgate |first2=Ken |last3=Wisner |first3=Ben |title=Taking the naturalness out of natural disasters |journal=Nature |date=April 1976 |volume=260 |issue=5552 |pages=566–567 |doi=10.1038/260566a0 |bibcode=1976Natur.260..566O |doi-access=free }}
* {{cite book |doi=10.4324/9781849771924 |title=Mapping Vulnerability |date=2013 |isbn=978-1-136-56162-7 |editor-last1=Bankoff |editor-last2=Frerks |editor-last3=Hilhorst |editor-first1=Greg |editor-first2=Georg |editor-first3=Dorothea }}
*Oliver-Smith, A. and S. M. Hoffman (2002). Theorizing Disasters: Nature, Power and Culture. Theorizing Disasters: Nature, Power and Culture (Catastrophe and Culture: The Anthropology of Disaster). A. Oliver-Smith. Santa Fe, School of American Research Press.
*{{cite journal |last1=Peek |first1=Lori |title=Children and Disasters: Understanding Vulnerability, Developing Capacities, and Promoting Resilience — An Introduction |journal=Children, Youth and Environments |date=2008 |volume=18 |issue=1 |pages=1–29 |doi=10.1353/cye.2008.0052 |jstor=10.7721/chilyoutenvi.18.1.0001 }}
* {{cite book |last1=Prowse |first1=Martin |title=Towards a Clearer Understanding of 'vulnerability' in Relation to Chronic Poverty |date=2003 |publisher=Chronic Poverty Research Centre |isbn=978-1-904049-23-4 }}
*{{cite journal |last1=Sánchez-González |first1=Diego |last2=Egea-Jiménez |first2=Carmen |title=Enfoque de vulnerabilidad social para investigar las desventajas socioambientales: Su aplicación en el estudio de los adultos mayores |trans-title=Social Vulnerability approach to investigate the social and environmental disadvantages. Its application in the study of elderly people |language=es |journal=Papeles de población |date=September 2011 |volume=17 |issue=69 |pages=151–185 |url=http://www.scielo.org.mx/scielo.php?script=sci_arttext&pid=S1405-74252011000300006 }}
*{{cite journal |last1=Stough |first1=Laura M. |last2=Sharp |first2=Amy N. |last3=Decker |first3=Curt |last4=Wilker |first4=Nachama |title=Disaster case management and individuals with disabilities |journal=Rehabilitation Psychology |date=2010 |volume=55 |issue=3 |pages=211–220 |doi=10.1037/a0020079 |pmid=20804264 |hdl=1969.1/153155 |hdl-access=free }}
*Villágran de León, J. C. (2006). "Vulnerability Assessment in the Context of Disaster-Risk, a Conceptual and Methodological Review."{{verify source|date=November 2020}}
*Warner, K. and T. Loster (2006). A research and action agenda for social vulnerability. Bonn, United Nations University Institute of Environment and Human Security.{{verify source|date=November 2020}}
*{{cite journal |last1=Watts |first1=Michael J. |last2=Bohle |first2=Hans G. |title=The space of vulnerability: the causal structure of hunger and famine |journal=Progress in Human Geography |date=March 1993 |volume=17 |issue=1 |pages=43–67 |doi=10.1177/030913259301700103 }}
*Wisner, B, Blaikie, P., T. Cannon, Davis, I. (2004). At Risk: Natural hazards, people's vulnerability and disasters. 2nd edition, London, Routledge.

===Further reading===
;Overview
*[[Neil Adger|Adger, W. Neil]]. 2006. Vulnerability. Global Environmental Change 16 (3):268–281.
*Cutter, Susan L., Bryan J. Boruff, and W. Lynn Shirley. 2003. Social vulnerability to environmental hazards. Social Science Quarterly 84 (2):242–261.
*Gallopín, Gilberto C. 2006. Linkages between vulnerability, resilience, and adaptive capacity. Global Environmental Change 16 (3):293–303.
*Oliver-Smith, Anthony. 2004. Theorizing vulnerability in a globalized world: a political ecological perspective. In Mapping vulnerability: disasters, development & people, edited by G. Bankoff, G. Frerks and D. Hilhorst. Sterling, VA: Earthscan, 10–24.

;Natural hazards paradigm
*Burton, Ian, [[Robert W. Kates]], and [[Gilbert F. White]]. 1993. The environment as hazard. 2nd ed. New York: Guilford Press.
*Kates, Robert W. 1971. Natural hazard in human ecological perspectives: hypotheses and models. Economic Geography 47 (3):438–451.
*Mitchell, James K. 2001. What's in a name?: issues of terminology and language in hazards research (Editorial). Environmental Hazards 2:87–88.

;Political-ecological tradition
*Blaikie, Piers, Terry Cannon, Ian Davis, and Ben Wisner. 1994. At risk: natural hazards, people's vulnerability, and disasters. ist ed. London: Routledge. (see below under Wisner for 2nd edition)
*Bohle, H. G., T. E. Downing, and M. J. Watts. 1994. Climate change and social vulnerability: the sociology and geography of food insecurity. Global Environmental Change 4:37–48.
*{{cite web |last1=Morel |first1=Raymond |title=L4D Learning for Democracy: Pre-industrial societies and strategies for the exploitation of resources: a theoretical framework for understanding why some settlements are resilient and some settlements are vulnerable to crisis – Daniel Curtis |url=http://www.ict-21.ch/l4d/pg/file/read/887467/preindustrial-societies-and-strategies-for-the-exploitation-of-resources-a-theoretical-framework-for-understanding-why-some-settlements-are-resilient-and-some-settlements-are-vulnerable-to-crisis-daniel-curtis }}
*Langridge, R.; J. Christian-Smith; and K.A. Lohse. "Access and Resilience: Analyzing the Construction of Social Resilience to the Threat of Water Scarcity" Ecology and Society 11(2): insight section.
*O'Brien, P., and Robin Leichenko. 2000. Double exposure: assessing the impacts of climate change within the context of economic globalization. Global Environmental Change 10 (3):221–232.
*Quarantelli, E. L. 1989. Conceptualizing disasters from a sociological perspective. International Journal of Mass Emergencies and Disasters 7 (3):243–251.
*Sarewitz, Daniel, Roger Pielke Jr., and Mojdeh Keykhah. 2003. Vulnerability and risk: some thoughts from a political and policy perspective. Risk Analysis 23 (4):805–810.
*Tierney, Kathleen J. 1999. Toward a critical sociology of risk. Sociological Forum 14 (2):215–242.
*Wisner, B., Blaikie, Piers, Terry Cannon, Ian Davis. 2004. At risk: natural hazards, people's vulnerability, and disasters. 2nd ed. London: Routledge.

;Human-ecological tradition
*Brooks, Nick, W. Neil Adger, and P. Mick Kelly. 2005. The determinants of vulnerability and adaptive capacity at the national level and the implications for adaptation. Global Environmental Change 15 (2):151–163.
*Comfort, L., Ben Wisner, Susan L. Cutter, R. Pulwarty, Kenneth Hewitt, Anthony Oliver-Smith, J. Wiener, M. Fordham, W. Peacock, and F. Krimgold. 1999. Reframing disaster policy: the global evolution of vulnerable communities. Environmental Hazards 1 (1):39–44.
*Cutter, Susan L. 1996. Vulnerability to environmental hazards. Progress in Human Geography 20 (4):529–539.
*Dow, Kirsten. 1992. Exploring differences in our common future(s): the meaning of vulnerability to global environmental change. Geoforum 23:417–436.
*[[Diana Liverman|Liverman, Diana]]. 1990. Vulnerability to global environmental change. In Understanding global environmental change: the contributions of risk analysis and management, edited by R. E. Kasperson, K. Dow, D. Golding and J. X. Kasperson. Worcester, MA: Clark University, 27–44.
*Peek, L., & Stough, L. M. (2010). Children with disabilities in the context of disaster: A social vulnerability perspective. Child Development, 81(4), 1260–1270.
*{{cite journal |last1=Turner |first1=B. L. |last2=Kasperson |first2=Roger E. |last3=Matson |first3=Pamela A. |last4=McCarthy |first4=James J. |last5=Corell |first5=Robert W. |last6=Christensen |first6=Lindsey |last7=Eckley |first7=Noelle |last8=Kasperson |first8=Jeanne X. |last9=Luers |first9=Amy |last10=Martello |first10=Marybeth L. |last11=Polsky |first11=Colin |last12=Pulsipher |first12=Alexander |last13=Schiller |first13=Andrew |title=A framework for vulnerability analysis in sustainability science |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=8 July 2003 |volume=100 |issue=14 |pages=8074–8079 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1231335100 |pmid=12792023 |pmc=166184 |doi-access=free }}

;Research Needs
*Cutter, Susan L. 2001. A research agenda for vulnerability science and environmental hazards [Internet]. [[International Human Dimensions Programme]] on Global Environmental Change [cited August 18, 2006]. Available from https://web.archive.org/web/20070213050141/http://www.ihdp.uni-bonn.de/html/publications/publications.html.
*{{cite journal |last1=Young |first1=Oran R. |last2=Berkhout |first2=Frans |last3=Gallopin |first3=Gilberto C. |last4=Janssen |first4=Marco A. |last5=Ostrom |first5=Elinor |last6=van der Leeuw |first6=Sander |title=The globalization of socio-ecological systems: An agenda for scientific research |journal=Global Environmental Change |date=1 August 2006 |volume=16 |issue=3 |pages=304–316 |doi=10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2006.03.004 |bibcode=2006GEC....16..304Y }}
* {{cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Ang |last2=Toll |first2=Mathew |last3=Bentley |first3=Rebecca |title=Mapping social vulnerability indicators to understand the health impacts of climate change: a scoping review |journal=The Lancet Planetary Health |date=November 2023 |volume=7 |issue=11 |pages=e925–e937 |doi=10.1016/S2542-5196(23)00216-4 |pmid=37940212 |doi-access=free }}
